parts.content: Define membrane potential and resting potential, and state a typical resting potential value in mV.
parts.markscheme:
- Membrane potential is the voltage (electrical potential) difference across a cell’s plasma membrane. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Resting potential is the membrane potential of a neuron when it is not transmitting an impulse/action potential. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Typical resting potential is about $-70\text{ mV}$ (accept $-60$ to $-80\text{ mV}$). <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Outline three factors that create the resting potential in a neuron.
parts.markscheme:
- Sodium–potassium pump transports $3\text{ Na}^+$ out and $2\text{ K}^+$ in (uses ATP), contributing to net negative charge inside. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Membrane is more permeable to $\text{K}^+$ than $\text{Na}^+$ (e.g., K$^+$ leak channels), so more $\text{K}^+$ diffuses out than $\text{Na}^+$ diffuses in. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Impermeant negatively charged ions/proteins inside the neuron contribute to the negative internal charge. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Voltage-gated $\text{Na}^+$ and $\text{K}^+$ channels are closed at rest (most permeability due to leak channels). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Electrochemical gradients for $\text{Na}^+$ and $\text{K}^+$ established/maintained by active transport and diffusion. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Analyze the specific roles played by cholesterol and glycoproteins in maintaining the integrity and function of the plasma membrane.
parts.markscheme:
- Cholesterol is an amphipathic molecule that embeds within the hydrophobic tail region of the phospholipid bilayer <marks>1 mark</marks>
- It regulates membrane fluidity by restricting the movement of phospholipids at high temperatures <marks>1 mark</marks>
- It prevents the membrane from solidifying/crystallizing at low temperatures by disrupting the regular packing of hydrocarbon chains <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Glycoproteins consist of carbohydrate chains covalently bonded to proteins on the extracellular surface <marks>1 mark</marks>
- They serve as cell-to-cell recognition markers (e.g., identifying self vs. non-self in the immune system) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- They act as receptors for chemical signals such as hormones or neurotransmitters <marks>1 mark</marks>
- They are involved in cell adhesion, helping cells stick together to form tissues <marks>1 mark</marks>
<note>Award maximum 4 marks</note>

parts.content: Detail the sequence of events in a single cycle of the sodium-potassium ($$Na^+/K^+$$) pump and explain how this contributes to the cell's electrochemical gradient.
parts.markscheme:
- The pump is an integral transmembrane protein with high affinity for sodium ions in its internal configuration <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Three sodium ions ($$3Na^+$$) bind to specific binding sites on the intracellular side of the pump <marks>1 mark</marks>
- The binding of $$Na^+$$ triggers the hydrolysis of $$ATP$$, which phosphorylates the pump (attaches a phosphate group) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Phosphorylation causes a conformational change (shape change) in the protein, translocating the $$Na^+$$ across the membrane <marks>1 mark</marks>
- The $$3Na^+$$ ions are released to the extracellular fluid, and the pump now has a high affinity for potassium ions <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Two potassium ions ($$2K^+$$) bind to the pump from the extracellular side, triggering the release of the phosphate group <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Dephosphorylation causes the pump to return to its original conformation, releasing the $$2K^+$$ into the cytoplasm <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Because more positive charge is moved out ($$3Na^+$$) than in ($$2K^+$$), the pump is electrogenic and establishes a negative internal membrane potential <marks>1 mark</marks>
<note>Award maximum 7 marks</note>

parts.content: Explain how the fluidity of the phospholipid bilayer enables the processes of endocytosis and exocytosis.
parts.markscheme:
- The fluid nature of the membrane is due to the weak hydrophobic interactions between the fatty acid tails, allowing phospholipids to move laterally <marks>1 mark</marks>
- This flexibility allows the plasma membrane to change shape and invaginate to form vesicles during endocytosis <marks>1 mark</marks>
- During exocytosis, the phospholipid bilayers of the secretory vesicle and the plasma membrane can merge/fuse because they are both fluid <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Fusion is followed by the spontaneous rearrangement of the bilayer to become continuous, releasing the vesicle's contents <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Fluidity ensures that the membrane can break and reform (re-seal) without losing its integrity or leaking cytoplasm <marks>1 mark</marks>
<note>Award maximum 4 marks</note>

parts.content: Explain why the action potential frequency does not increase beyond 50 Hz.

parts.content: Using Panel B of the figure, describe the role of calcium in neurotransmitter release at the presynaptic membrane.
parts.markscheme:
- Calcium enters via voltage-gated Ca$^{2+}$ channels
- Calcium acts as a signal inside the presynaptic terminal
- Calcium triggers vesicle fusion with the membrane

<marks>2 marks</marks>

<Note>Award full marks for "Any two".</Note>

steps.title: Calcium entry via channels
steps.content: The process begins when an action potential reaches the presynaptic terminal. This electrical impulse causes the **voltage-gated $Ca^{2+}$ channels** shown in Panel B to open. Because the concentration of calcium is higher outside the cell, $Ca^{2+}$ ions flow into the presynaptic neuron.

steps.title: Calcium acts as signal
steps.content: Once inside the cytoplasm of the presynaptic terminal, the calcium ions act as an **internal signal** (or second messenger). The sudden increase in local $Ca^{2+}$ concentration is the biological trigger that initiates the movement of neurotransmitter-filled vesicles toward the release site.

steps.title: Triggering vesicle fusion
steps.content: In the final stage of this sequence, the calcium signal causes the **synaptic vesicles to fuse** with the presynaptic membrane. As shown in Panel B, molecular machinery like **SNAP25** and **Syntaxin1** helps pull the vesicle and membrane together. This fusion opens the vesicle, allowing neurotransmitters to be released into the synaptic cleft via exocytosis.

parts.content: Based on Panel A of the figure, explain how vesicles are prepared before neurotransmitter release.
parts.markscheme:
- Vesicles dock at the presynaptic membrane
OR
- Vesicles are primed by SNARE proteins
OR
- Vesicles are prepared by assembling release machinery

<marks>2 marks</marks>

<Note>Award full marks for "Any two".</Note>

steps.title: Analyze the sequential process
steps.content: To understand how vesicles are prepared, we look at the first two stages shown in Panel A of the diagram. Before a vesicle can release neurotransmitters, it must undergo a specific sequence of physical and molecular changes to become "fusion-ready."

steps.title: Step 1: Docking
steps.content: The first preparatory stage is **docking**. In this stage, the neurotransmitter-filled vesicle moves toward the presynaptic membrane and makes its initial physical contact. It is positioned at a specific site called the active zone, putting it in close proximity to the site of $Ca^{2+}$ entry.

steps.title: Step 2: Priming
steps.content: The second stage is **priming**. This is a maturation step where the vesicle becomes physically tethered and tightly associated with the membrane. During priming, the "release machinery" mentioned in the diagram (such as SNARE proteins) begins to assemble, ensuring the vesicle is ready to fuse immediately when a calcium signal arrives.

steps.title: Summary of preparation
steps.content:
Based on the markscheme, to earn the **2 marks**, you need to identify two distinct parts of this preparation:

1.  **Vesicles dock** at the presynaptic membrane.
2.  **Vesicles are primed** by SNARE proteins (or the assembly of the release machinery).

steps.title: Initial Docking at Membrane
steps.content: Before neurotransmitters can be released, vesicles containing the chemical signals must first undergo **docking**. In this stage, the vesicle moves from the cytoplasm toward the presynaptic membrane and physically attaches to a specific site. This ensures the vesicle is in the correct location, directly adjacent to the membrane where fusion will occur.

steps.title: Priming and SNARE Assembly
steps.content: Once docked, the vesicle must be **primed** to become "fusion-competent." This is a biochemical process where the **release machinery** is assembled. Specifically, **SNARE proteins** located on both the vesicle and the presynaptic membrane (such as **Syntaxin1** and **SNAP25**) interact and entwine. This molecular assembly pulls the vesicle extremely close to the membrane, effectively "cocking the trigger" so that the vesicle is ready to fuse the moment calcium ions ($Ca^{2+}$) enter the terminal.

steps.title: Final Summary
steps.content:
In summary, the preparation involves two key marks in the markscheme:
1.  **Docking** the vesicles at the presynaptic membrane.
2.  **Priming** the vesicles through the assembly of the SNARE protein complex.

This biochemical preparation is essential for the rapid, synchronous release of neurotransmitters seen in the graphs in Panel C.

parts.content: Suggest one reason why spontaneous neurotransmitter release is important, even when no action potential is present.
parts.markscheme:
- Maintains baseline synaptic activity
OR
- Ensures synaptic readiness

<marks>1 mark</marks>

<Note>Any one</Note>

steps.title: Define spontaneous release
steps.content: Even when a neuron is not firing an action potential, small amounts of neurotransmitter are occasionally released into the synaptic cleft. As shown in **Panel C**, this is called **spontaneous release**. Unlike 'evoked' release, which is a massive surge triggered by a signal, spontaneous release is a quiet, continuous process.

steps.title: Maintain baseline activity
steps.content: From a physiological maintenance perspective, synapses require a constant 'trickle' of activity to remain healthy and stable. This **baseline synaptic activity** prevents the synapse from becoming dormant. Without this low-level stimulation, the post-synaptic receptors might degrade or the connection might weaken over time.

steps.title: Ensure synaptic readiness
steps.content: Spontaneous release also ensures **synaptic readiness**. By having vesicles occasionally dock, prime, and fuse (as seen in **Panel A**), the molecular machinery remains active and 'warmed up.' This ensures that when a real action potential finally arrives, the synapse is prepared to respond immediately and efficiently.

steps.title: State the final reason
steps.content: According to the markscheme, you only need to provide one reason. The most common explanations are that it **maintains baseline synaptic activity** or **ensures synaptic readiness** for future signals.

steps.title: Identify spontaneous release
steps.content: Looking at **Panel C**, we can see that even when no stimulus or action potential is present, there is a low level of background neurotransmitter release. This is known as spontaneous release, which results in small, random electrical signals in the postsynaptic neuron.

steps.title: Maintaining molecular machinery
steps.content:
Using the **Operational Readiness Approach**, we can view the synapse as a system that needs to stay 'active' to be efficient. Spontaneous release ensures that the molecular machinery shown in **Panel B** (like SNAP25 and Syntaxin1) and the docking/priming process in **Panel A** remain functional. 

By constantly cycling a small number of vesicles, the cell ensures the 'fusion pores' and transport mechanisms don't become stagnant or stuck, keeping the presynaptic terminal physically ready for a sudden influx of $Ca^{2+}$.

steps.title: Priming postsynaptic receptors
steps.content: Spontaneous release also keeps the postsynaptic receptors 'primed.' Without any neurotransmitter stimulation, these receptors might be down-regulated (removed from the membrane) or become less sensitive. This background activity ensures that when a real action potential arrives, the postsynaptic membrane is chemically prepared to respond instantly.

steps.title: Final Conclusion
steps.content:
Therefore, the primary reason for this activity is to **ensure synaptic readiness**. It keeps the entire chemical and mechanical bridge between neurons in a state of immediate operational availability.

**Marking Note:** To earn the mark, you simply need to state that it maintains readiness or baseline activity.

The question asks us to identify a specific stage in the life cycle of a synaptic vesicle based on the provided diagram. Specifically, we need the term for when the vesicle is "ready but not yet fused."

By visually following the arrows in the diagram, we can see that the stage occurring after **docking** but before **fusion** is labeled as **Priming**.

In biological terms, "priming" refers to the ATP-dependent process that makes the docked vesicle competent for rapid, calcium-triggered fusion.

Based on the visual evidence in the flowchart, the term for the stage when the vesicle is ready but not yet fused is the **Priming stage**.

questions.specification:
Which of the following distinguishes **nervous** from **hormonal** coordination?

| Characteristic        | Nervous system | Endocrine system |
|------------------------|----------------|------------------|
| Speed of signal        | Fast           | Slow             |
| Duration of effect     | Short-term     | Long-term        |
| Type of signal         | Electrical     | Chemical         |

labeledSubSubTopics.path: C - Interaction and Interdependence > C3.1 Integration of body systems > C3.1.1 System integration
labeledSubSubTopics.title: C3.1.1 System integration
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question compares the nervous and endocrine systems based on their functional characteristics (speed, duration, and signal type). These are the two primary systems responsible for system integration and coordination in the human body, making 'System integration' the most appropriate topic.

options.content: Only signal speed differs
All three features differ as shown
Hormonal control is faster and more direct
Nervous control is slower but more precise
